topic: A person finds out their biography was written without their knowledge
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crisp, cream-colored paper felt alien beneath my fingertips. I traced the elegant script, the carefully chosen anecdotes, the precise chronology of my life. Every sentence was a carefully curated piece of me, yet none of it belonged to me. A cold, heavy weight settled in my chest, making each breath shallow. I reread the section detailing my 'defining triumph,' the description so vivid it felt like watching someone else's highlight reel. My hands began to tremble, the tremor escalating until the paper threatened to slip from my grasp. I sank onto the edge of the antique desk, the silence of the study amplifying the frantic hammering in my ribs. Who had done this? The realization wasn't a sudden shock, but a slow, sickening realization that the foundation of my existence had been built on borrowed bricks. I stared at the ink, the sheer audacity of the deception making my throat constrict.

[story 2]

It was late, the only light in the room a weak, amber glow spilling from the desk lamp onto the documents. I had been searching for an old letter, something trivial, and instead, I unearthed this—a meticulously crafted narrative of my existence. My jaw tightened until my teeth ached. I felt a profound emptiness bloom in my stomach, a hollow space where ownership should have resided. I picked up a pen, intending to cross out a line, but my hand shook too violently. I dropped it onto the blotter with a soft thud. A low, ragged sound escaped my lips, more of a choked sigh than a word. The air seemed thick, suffocating. I leaned back in my chair, pressing the heels of my hands against my eyes, trying to force the image into a shape that made sense, but it remained fractured, a mosaic of someone else's achievements.

[story 3]

The revelation hit me not like a physical blow, but like the sudden extinguishing of a light source. I sat bolt upright, the chair scraping loudly against the hardwood floor. The biographical sketch lay spread out, mocking me with its flawless artistry. My vision tunneled; the edges of the room blurred into indistinct shapes. I began to pace, a restless, jerky motion that betrayed a deep internal turmoil. Each step was heavy, burdened by an inexplicable sense of trespass. I stopped by the window, looking out at the indifferent city below, the noise of traffic a dull roar against the sudden, overwhelming silence inside my head. I curled my fingers into tight fists, the tension radiating up my arms, a silent plea for understanding that no one could possibly hear.
